comment.body: "Speculation is always fascinating, and it can be a lot of fun while you are ahead of the game. If you want to try your luck at it, put aside a portion— the smaller the better—of your capital in a separate fund for this purpose. Never add more money to this account just because the market has gone up and profits are rolling in. (That’s the time to think of taking money out of your speculative fund.) Never mingle your speculative and investment operations in the same account, nor in any part of your thinking."

comment.body:
From ZoomyZoom and Claude interpretation - The AI Money Flow Problem:
What's happening:
You pay $200 → Cursor gets $200
Cursor pays $500 to OpenAI (losing $300, covered by VC money)
OpenAI pays $1000 to AWS (losing $500, covered by VC money)
AWS pays $10,000 to NVIDIA for chips
The "House of Cards" Problem:
Everyone's losing money except NVIDIA - Only the chip maker profits while everyone else burns cash
VC subsidies everywhere - The whole chain only works because venture capitalists are funding the losses at every level
Upside-down economics - Each company pays MORE for their input costs than they collect from customers
NVIDIA captures all the value - They're the only ones with pricing power and real profits. The AI industry works the same way - it's entirely dependent on VC funding to subsidize losses at every level. When that funding dries up, companies either need to:
Raise prices dramatically (killing demand)
Cut costs drastically (hurting quality)
Go out of business
That's why it's a "house of cards" - remove the VC subsidy, and the whole structure collapses.

comment.body:
"All in all: a big opportunity is coming in the near future…" -- Alma in "Playing the AI Bubble" 

For investors, I think Alma also gave us many useful info in her articles. A sign (perhaps still early) that I recognize, is that companies backed by Peter Thiel are going public, i.e., Palantir, Figma etc., so the distribution to retails have begun while insiders are cashing out.

comment.body:
The hyperscalers like Microsoft essentially sell computing power of the data centers, which is used by companies closer to the applications of AI (LLM, softwares etc.). Their stock prices are not cheap now. 

Now companies like Snowflake, Cloudflare, etc. are not yet GAAP profitable, nor is Open AI; Palantir is profitable but with extreme valuation; and also AI robotics is still in infancy... I don't doubt that AI is a powerful technology, but there are still some gaps to fill. One wants to see how sustainable the CapEx is, whether energy, industrial capacity and the environment can support the rapid level of expansion of data centers... 

So there is a lot of speculative element, and speculations can still be right sometimes...

comment.body:
As an investor, I am concerned about the valuation cf the Buffect indicator. 

However, based on Alma's analysis, she is leaned towards the scenario of a further inflated bubble and valuation distortion, before an eventual washout and reshuffling... Right now doesn't seem the best time to buy these heated AI stocks; of course one can still trade based on momentum...
